step.title: Step 49
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blue block moved DOWN 6 times (from rows 20-23 to rows 44-47), but then stopped at the 7th DOWN action. So the blue block can move DOWN to rows 44-47, but not further.
The yellow arches are at rows 52-59. So the blue block is blocked at rows 48-51, which is 4 rows above the yellow arches.
This confirms there's an invisible obstacle at rows 48-51 that spans the entire width of the board.
But wait, the blue block is at cols 24-43, which overlaps with the yellow arches at cols 16-27 (left) and 40-51 (right). The blue block's left portion (cols 24-27) overlaps with the left yellow arch, and the right portion (cols 40-43) overlaps with the right yellow arch.
Maybe the blue block is blocked because it would overlap with the yellow arches if it moved DOWN further. But the yellow arches are at rows 52-59, and the blue block would be at rows 48-51. So they wouldn't overlap.
Unless... the yellow arches extend upward beyond row 52. Let me check the ascii representation to see the exact shape of the yellow arches.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me move the blue block to a position where it doesn't overlap with the yellow arches horizontally, and see if it can move DOWN further.
The yellow arches are at cols 16-27 (left) and 40-51 (right). The gap is at cols 28-39. If I position the blue block entirely within the gap (cols 28-39), it wouldn't overlap with the yellow arches.
But the blue block is 20 cols wide, and the gap is only 12 cols wide. So the blue block can't fit entirely within the gap.
However, if I position the blue block at cols 20-39, the left portion (cols 20-27) would overlap with the left yellow arch (cols 16-27), and the right portion (cols 28-39) would be within the gap.
If I position the blue block at cols 28-47, the left portion (cols 28-39) would be within the gap, and the right portion (cols 40-47) would overlap with the right yellow arch (cols 40-51).
So no matter where I position the blue block horizontally, it would overlap with at least one yellow arch if it moves DOWN to rows 48-51 or below.
But the yellow arches are at rows 52-59, so the blue block at rows 48-51 shouldn't overlap with them.
Unless... the yellow arches extend upward beyond row 52. Let me check the ascii representation to see the exact shape of the yellow arches.
Actually, let me try moving the blue block to the far left (cols 0-19) and see if it can move DOWN further. At cols 0-19, the blue block wouldn't overlap with the yellow arches (cols 16-27 and 40-51) except for cols 16-19, which is a small overlap.
Wait, the blue block at cols 0-19 would overlap with the left yellow arch at cols 16-19 (4 cols). So there's still an overlap.
Let me try moving the blue block to cols 48-63 (far right) and see if it can move DOWN further. At cols 48-63, the blue block would overlap with the right yellow arch at cols 48-51 (4 cols).
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Let me check if the blue block can move DOWN when it's at cols 48-63 (far right).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move blue block to far right and try moving DOWN
# Current: rows 44-47, cols 24-43
# Move RIGHT to get to cols 48-63 (need to move RIGHT 6, but that might go off board)
# Let's try RIGHT 3 first to get to cols 36-55

action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blue = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
print(f"Blue after RIGHT 3: boundary={blue['boundary'][:4]}")

# Try moving DOWN
action(['DOWN'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blue2 = [n for n in seg2 if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
print(f"Blue after DOWN: boundary={blue2['boundary'][:4]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
